COLUMBUS, OH 43201

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
citations.narrative:
Twice daily observations performed by facility staff failed to identify that a cynomolgus macaque sustained an injury
caused by a collar that did not fit properly. The collar was placed on the macaque on 5/27/25. On the first day of the
study, 9/30/25, a technician observed that the collar appeared snug and likely needed to be changed and a veterinarian
was notified. After dosing, the animal was sedated for an examination and collar change. During the examination, the
veterinarian discovered sores on the back (dorsal) part of the neck which were only visible once the collar was removed.
The hair was shaved and the sores were cleaned. The macaque was treated with analgesics and topical medication. After
conference with the veterinarian and the study director, the macaque was removed from study participation. It was noted
upon review of animal records that from the time the collar was placed to the time of the discovery of the wounds, the
animal was observed to be eating and gaining weight at a normal and predictable rate. There was no previous indication
of discomfort or distress observed prior to the examination under sedation.
Each research facility shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily observation of
all animals to assess their health and well-being.
The facility identified and put corrective actions in place by having the collar fit assessed on all primates in the study room
on 9/30/25, and having a veterinarian assess the fit for all primates in the facility by 10/1/25. Additionally, regular fit
checks have been scheduled for all primate studies at regular intervals going forward, and the animal care staff have
been re-trained on assessing collar fitness prior to today’s inspection.
Correct by: February 24, 2026, onward

Two rhesus macaques escaped their enclosure. Upon touring the facility, documentation was obtained that showed on
2/15/26, two rhesus macaques housed in the same enclosure were found freely moving around the room by animal
caretakers in the morning. The room housed a total of 11 macaques. A vivarium employee was able to confirm for the
inspector that the macaques left their primary enclosure sometime during the night due to a lock meant to secure the
catch pan not being properly attached. The lock is a secondary means to ensure that the primates could not remove the
pan after clicking the latch that is adhered to the rack by the manufacturer.
Primary enclosures that do not securely contain the animal can lead to animal escapes, stress, or injuries. Animal
enclosures must be maintained so that they contain the non-human primates securely and prevent accidental opening of
the enclosure, including opening by the animal.
The facility identified and corrected this issue prior to today’s inspection and provided additional employee training. From
Feb. 24, 2026, onward, the facility will continue to ensure the primary enclosures are maintained in such a manner that
they will safely contain the macaques and prevent the accidental opening of an enclosure.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.33(b)(3)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care. 
Twice daily observations performed by facility staff failed to identify that a cynomolgus macaque sustained an injury 
caused by a collar that did not fit properly. The collar was placed on the macaque on 5/27/25. On the first day of the 
study, 9/30/25, a technician observed that the collar appeared snug and likely needed to be changed and a veterinarian 
was notified. After dosing, the animal was sedated for an examination and collar change. During the examination, the 
veterinarian discovered sores on the back (dorsal) part of the neck which were only visible once the collar was removed. 
The hair was shaved and the sores were cleaned. The macaque was treated with analgesics and topical medication. After 
conference with the veterinarian and the study director, the macaque was removed from study participation. It was noted 
upon review of animal records that from the time the collar was placed to the time of the discovery of the wounds, the 
animal was observed to be eating and gaining weight at a normal and predictable rate. There was no previous indication 
of discomfort or distress observed prior to the examination under sedation.  
 
Each research facility shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily observation of 
all animals to assess their health and well-being.  
 
The facility identified and put corrective actions in place by having the collar fit assessed on all primates in the study room 
on 9/30/25, and having a veterinarian assess the fit for all primates in the facility by 10/1/25. Additionally, regular fit 
checks have been scheduled for all primate studies at regular intervals going forward, and the animal care staff have 
been re-trained on assessing collar fitness prior to today’s inspection.  
 
Correct by: February 24, 2026, onward 
3.80(a)(2)(iii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
Two rhesus macaques escaped their enclosure. Upon touring the facility, documentation was obtained that showed on 
2/15/26, two rhesus macaques housed in the same enclosure were found freely moving around the room by animal 
caretakers in the morning. The room housed a total of 11 macaques. A vivarium employee was able to confirm for the 
inspector that the macaques left their primary enclosure sometime during the night due to a lock meant to secure the

catch pan not being properly attached. The lock is a secondary means to ensure that the primates could not remove the 
pan after clicking the latch that is adhered to the rack by the manufacturer.  
 
Primary enclosures that do not securely contain the animal can lead to animal escapes, stress, or injuries. Animal 
enclosures must be maintained so that they contain the non-human primates securely and prevent accidental opening of 
the enclosure, including opening by the animal.  
 
The facility identified and corrected this issue prior to today’s inspection and provided additional employee training. From 
Feb. 24, 2026, onward, the facility will continue to ensure the primary enclosures are maintained in such a manner that 
they will safely contain the macaques and prevent the accidental opening of an enclosure. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
